content.text: In an agentic AI microservices architecture, multiple services collaborate to deliver intelligent behavior: an orchestration service manages the agent loop, a prompt service serves versioned templates, a tool service executes external actions, a model gateway manages provider routing, and an evaluation service measures quality. When ownership boundaries between these services are ambiguous, cross-team changes become collision-prone, on-call responses misfire to the wrong team, and prompt changes silently violate tool-contract assumptions. Clear ownership boundaries, encoded in CODEOWNERS files and service catalogs, are the structural solution.

Ownership boundaries in agentic AI microservices are explicit assignments of responsibility for each service, its configuration files, its schemas, and its shared contracts to a specific team. In practice, these boundaries are encoded in four places: the CODEOWNERS file (which controls who must review PRs touching each service's code), the service catalog (which records the owning team, on-call rotation, and runbook location), the OpenAPI or AsyncAPI schema (which defines the contract boundary between services), and the shared library ownership policy (which determines who can merge changes to shared data types used by multiple services).

For agentic AI systems specifically, ownership extends beyond the application code to cover three additional artifact types: the prompt templates that the prompt service serves, the evaluation datasets that the evaluation service runs, and the tool schemas that the tool service exposes. Each of these artifact types has a different review profile, and the ownership model must accommodate that diversity.

Agentic AI microservices have unusually high coupling at the behavioral level. A prompt service change that modifies the expected output format of a tool call will break the orchestration service's parser, even if no API schema changes. A tool service change that adds a new error mode will cause the orchestration service's retry logic to fail silently if it does not handle the new mode. These behavioral contracts are invisible to conventional microservice coupling analysis, which focuses only on API schemas.

Ownership boundaries make these behavioral dependencies explicit. When the prompt service (owned by the ML team) wants to change the expected JSON structure in a tool call instruction, CODEOWNERS requires the platform team (which owns the orchestration service and the tool service) to review and approve the change. This cross-team review gate catches behavioral mismatches before they reach production.
Prevents silent behavioral mismatches: cross-ownership review gates catch contract violations before deployment.
Correct on-call routing: incidents are routed to the team that owns the affected service rather than to a generic ops queue.
Reduced merge conflicts: each team works primarily in its own service directories with clear merge authority.
Accountability clarity: every service has a named owner who is responsible for its SLA, documentation, and deprecation lifecycle.
Enables parallel development: teams work independently on their services because ownership boundaries define stable interfaces between them.
Security alignment: each service's security posture is owned by the team most qualified to assess it.
Cross-team coordination overhead: changes that span multiple services require reviews from multiple teams, slowing delivery.
Shared library deadlock: when a shared data type is owned by one team but needed by another, changes require negotiation.
Service boundary drift: teams may gradually accrete ownership of behavior that belongs in another service, eroding the original boundary.
On-call knowledge silos: teams deeply familiar with their own service may not understand adjacent services, complicating cross-service incidents.
Documentation maintenance: service catalog entries and CODEOWNERS files must be actively maintained to reflect team reorganisations.

Ownership boundaries work through a combination of repository structure, CODEOWNERS configuration, and service catalog registration. The repository structure places each microservice in its own top-level directory (or, in a polyrepo, its own repository). The CODEOWNERS file maps each directory to the owning team. The service catalog records additional metadata: the team name, Slack channel, on-call policy, runbook URL, and dependency graph.

For shared artifacts (the OpenAPI schema file for a tool API that both the orchestration service and the tool service must agree on), ownership is modelled as co-ownership in CODEOWNERS: both the orchestration team and the platform team must approve any change to the schema file. This is the primary mechanism for enforcing behavioral contracts between services.

attrs.alt: Ownership boundaries diagram for agentic AI microservices showing team assignments per service

content.text: The five core services in an agentic AI microservices architecture have distinct ownership profiles. The orchestration service and tool service are owned by the platform team. The prompt service and evaluation service are owned by the ML team, with the evaluation service carrying co-ownership by the safety team for its safety evaluation suites. The model gateway is co-owned by the ML team (which decides routing policy) and the platform team (which operates the infrastructure). Shared contracts between services are co-owned by all services that depend on them.
Any agentic AI system with more than two teams contributing code or prompts.
Systems where behavioral contracts between services (expected prompt output formats, tool schema conventions) need explicit review gates.
Organisations where on-call rotations are team-specific and must map to service ownership.
Repositories or polyrepos where multiple teams accidentally step on each other's changes due to unclear directory ownership.
Teams preparing for regulatory compliance that requires documented ownership of AI components.

# Monorepo CODEOWNERS with service-level ownership

# Top-level: DevOps owns root configuration
/package.json                        @org/devops-team
/.github/                            @org/devops-team
/docker-compose.yml                  @org/devops-team

# Orchestration service: platform team
/services/orchestration/             @org/platform-team

# Prompt service: ML team (with shared schema co-owned by platform)
/services/prompt/                    @org/ml-team
/services/prompt/src/                @org/ml-team
/services/prompt/prompts/            @org/ml-team @org/prompt-reviewers
/services/prompt/schema/             @org/ml-team @org/platform-team

# Tool service: platform + security teams
/services/tools/                     @org/platform-team @org/security-team
/services/tools/sandbox/             @org/security-team

# Model gateway: co-owned by ML and platform
/services/model-gateway/             @org/ml-team @org/platform-team
/services/model-gateway/routing/     @org/ml-team
/services/model-gateway/infra/       @org/platform-team

# Evaluation service: ML + safety co-ownership
/services/evaluation/                @org/ml-team
/services/evaluation/safety-evals/   @org/ml-team @org/safety-team

# Shared contracts (co-owned by all dependent teams)
/contracts/tool-call-schema.json     @org/ml-team @org/platform-team
/contracts/prompt-api.yaml           @org/ml-team @org/platform-team

# service-catalog.yaml - Backstage-compatible service catalog
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Component
metadata:
  name: prompt-service
  title: Prompt Service
  description: Serves versioned prompt templates to the orchestration layer
  annotations:
    github.com/project-slug: org/agentic-ai-platform
    pagerduty.com/service-id: P7X9ABC
    runbook: https://wiki.company.com/runbooks/prompt-service
  tags:
    - ai
    - prompts
    - versioning
spec:
  type: service
  lifecycle: production
  owner: group:ml-team
  system: agentic-ai-platform
  dependsOn:
    - component:orchestration-service
    - component:evaluation-service
  providesApis:
    - prompt-registry-api
---
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: API
metadata:
  name: prompt-registry-api
  title: Prompt Registry API
spec:
  type: openapi
  lifecycle: production
  owner: group:ml-team
  definition:
    $text: ./services/prompt/schema/openapi.yaml
A cross-service change scenario: the ML team wants to update the tool-calling prompt to ask the model to return structured JSON with a new

The CODEOWNERS configuration requires review from both teams. The ML team reviews the prompt change; the platform team reviews the schema change and the parser update. This three-way review ensures that the new confidence field is consistently defined in the schema, correctly populated by the prompt, and correctly parsed by the orchestration service.

A document intelligence company running an agentic AI pipeline (OCR → classification → extraction → summarisation) initially had all five stages owned by a single team. As the team grew, they split into ML team (responsible for prompts and model selection) and platform team (responsible for orchestration, tools, and infrastructure).

Without documented ownership boundaries, cross-team PRs caused repeated conflicts. The ML team would change a prompt's expected output format without telling the platform team, breaking the downstream parser. After implementing service-level CODEOWNERS and a service catalog, cross-team reviews caught three breaking changes before they merged. The mean time to restore service after production incidents dropped from 45 minutes to 12 minutes because PagerDuty was correctly routing alerts to the owning team.
assigning the same team to every service directory defeats the purpose of ownership boundaries. Be explicit about which team owns which service.
the API schemas and shared data types that bridge services are the highest-risk files. They require co-ownership by all teams that use them.
if the team listed in CODEOWNERS is different from the team on call for a service, incidents route to the wrong people.
Treating prompt files as generic config:
prompts are behavior-defining artifacts, not infrastructure configuration. They require ML team ownership, not DevOps team ownership.
an outdated service catalog that lists a disbanded team as the owner of a service renders the catalog useless during incidents.
Define service ownership boundaries before splitting a monolith; retrofitting is much harder once shared state has accumulated.
Co-own shared contracts (API schemas, shared data types) explicitly in CODEOWNERS rather than leaving ownership implicit.
Maintain a service catalog (Backstage, internal wiki, or YAML file) that records the owning team, on-call policy, and runbook for each service.
Align CODEOWNERS ownership with PagerDuty or OpsGenie service ownership so incidents route correctly without manual configuration.
Run a monthly 'ownership audit' that validates CODEOWNERS entries against current team membership and flags orphaned entries.
Document the behavioral contracts between services (expected prompt output formats, tool schema conventions) in the shared contracts directory.
Use branch protection with mandatory CODEOWNERS approval and dismiss stale reviews to prevent rubber-stamping after significant changes.

Ownership boundaries have no runtime performance impact; they are purely a development-time governance mechanism. At scale, the main overhead is review latency when cross-team changes require multiple team approvals. Mitigate this with well-defined service interfaces that minimise cross-boundary changes, and with a shared contract review process that batches interface negotiations into scheduled sync meetings rather than requiring ad-hoc review on every PR.

Ownership boundaries are a security control for agentic AI systems. Requiring security team co-ownership of the tool service prevents a developer from adding a new external API tool without security review. The same principle applies to the model gateway: any change that routes requests to a new model provider requires ML team approval, preventing unauthorised model adoption.

Reliability is improved through clear accountability. When the evaluation service alerts on a score regression, the co-owning ML and safety teams both receive the alert and share responsibility for the response. This prevents incidents from being ignored because each team assumed the other was handling it.
Assign each microservice to a named owner team in CODEOWNERS to enforce domain-expert review on every change.
Shared contracts (API schemas, tool call formats) must be co-owned by all services that depend on them.
Align CODEOWNERS ownership with on-call rotations so incidents route automatically to the right team.
Prompt templates and evaluation datasets require ML team ownership, not generic DevOps or platform team ownership.
Maintain a service catalog alongside CODEOWNERS to capture ownership metadata (runbook, on-call policy) beyond what CODEOWNERS provides.
Audit ownership entries monthly to keep CODEOWNERS accurate as teams grow, shrink, and reorganise.
